notes.anchor: all of Caesar's acts, writings, sayings, promises, and intentions
notes.note: The dead dictator's acta, ratified wholesale by the Senate on 17 March; Cicero's bitter complaint is that even Caesar's mere unwritten "intentions" are now treated as binding law.

notes.anchor: the praetors ought to summon the Senate to the Capitoline
notes.note: Brutus and Cassius, as urban and peregrine praetors of 44, had the right to convene the Senate; Cicero recalls urging them to do so at once on the day of the assassination, while the conspirators held the Capitol.
The festival of Liber, 17 March 44, when the amnesty meeting in the Temple of Tellus confirmed Caesar's acts; Atticus had evidently faulted the decisions taken that day.

notes.anchor: slaves and beggars were set loose on our houses with torches
notes.note: The mob violence after Caesar's funeral pyre in the Forum; "slaves" renders servique (the common people's rabble), which is not a reference to the jurist Servius Sulpicius and is left unbound.

notes.anchor: the Tebassi, the Scaevae, the Fangones
notes.note: Contemptuous plurals lumping together Antony's veteran land-grabbers and profiteers as so many "Tebassi, Scaevas, and Fangos"; these are types, not individuals, so "Scaevae" is deliberately not bound to any registered Scaeva.

notes.anchor: about Censorinus, about Messalla, about Plancus, about Postumus
notes.note: A roll of men profiting from Caesar's confirmed acts and the unsettled estates; the individual names are bound where the registry allows (Censorinus, Messalla Corvinus, Munatius Plancus, Curtius Postumus), with several binds tentative given the bare nomina.

The young heir C. Octavius (the future Augustus), landed in Italy and now at Naples; the next morning Balbus reports that he means to accept Caesar's inheritance, presaging the breach with Antony Cicero notes here.
The long-running affair of Buthrotum in Epirus, where Atticus had estates threatened by Caesar's planned veteran colony; Cicero promises to keep it in hand, as he must for his friend's sake.
The property inherited from M. Cluvius of Puteoli, whose yearly yield Cicero tracks; he reports it nearing a hundred thousand sesterces, with eighty thousand already skimmed off in the first year.
Pomponia, Atticus's sister and the divorced wife of Quintus Cicero the elder; young Quintus's new deference to her, after past hostility, is what so angers his father.
